coredata.dc:title: Solid-state fluorescent nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots with yellow emission for white light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Bingli
Liu, Zhiduo
Chen, Da
Gao, Bo
Yang, Yongsheng
Guo, Qinglei
Wang, Gang
coredata.dc:description: Light-emitting graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are widely investigated due to their distinct merits. However, GQDs generally suffer from aggregation-induced luminescence quenching, which means they are highly emissive in a solution state with uniform dispersion but dramatically quenched in a solid or aggregated state. This problem significantly limits the application of GQDs, partially in the solid-state light-emitting devices. In this report, we successfully developed a simple and efficient hydrothermal method for the production of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with strong solid-state fluorescence (SSF) by using citric acid and o-Phenylenediamine as precursors. Under the 365 nm UV light illumination, the produced N-GQDs in an aqueous state exhibit blue color, with a quantum yield of 58%. As the concentration of N-GQDs increases, the photoluminescence exhibits an obvious red-shift from blue to yellow. For the N-GQDs in solid state, yellow luminescence with a high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 28% is achieved under the 405 nm excitation. Finally, via the simple adjustment of thickness and the concentration of N-GQDs in blue emitting InGaN chips, color converter is enabled by constructing white light-emitting diode (WLED) device with improved color rendering index (CRI) and correlated color temperature (CCT).
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-GQDs
Solid-state fluorescence
White light-emitting diode
Color rendering index
Color temperature

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the preparation of N-GQDs. (b) TEM images of N-GQDs. The inset images are size distribution (bottom) and HRTEM image (right). (c) AFM image of the N-GQDs. (d) The height distribution of N-GQDs. (e) FT-IR spectrum of N-GQDs. The inset is the schematic of the speculated N-GQDs structure. High-resolution XPS: (f) C 1 s spectrum, (g) N 1s spectrum, and (h) O 1s spectrum of the N-GQDs.

(a) Absorption (blue), PL excitation (red) and PL emission (light blue) spectra of N-GQDs solution. (b) PL spectra of the N-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Time-resolved PL spectra of the N-GQDs. (d) Images of N-GQDs solution and N-GQDs powder under the UV illumination. (e) Excitation-emission matrix of the N-GQDs solution. (f) Excitation-emission matrix of the N-GQDs powder. (g) Normalized PL spectra and (h) the corresponding fluorescent images of N-GQDs solutions at various concentrations, i.e., 1, 20, 40, 80, 120, 160, and 200 mg/mL (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) Schematics of the energy transfer that occurs in the aggregated N-GQDs with different concentration levels. (b) Absorption spectra of N-GQDs in the solution state with different concentrations. (c) (αhν)2 vs hν curve of N-GQDs solutions with different concentrations. The gray dotted lines are the tangents of the curves. The intersection value on the X-axis is the band gap. (d) Energy band level of N-GQDs with different concentrations. (e) UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of N-GQDs (200 mg/mL). (f) Fluorescence lifetime spectra of N-GQDs solutions at various concentrations, i.e., 1, 10, 20, 40, 80, 120, 160 and 200 mg/mL. (g) Schematic of the mechanism for the self-quenching-resistant of as-prepared solid-state N-GQDs.

(a) Schematic of the WLED. (b) EL spectrum of the blue LED chip. Insets are images of the LED at on and off states. (c) CIE chromaticity diagram containing color coordinates of cool WLED, WLED and warm WLED. (d) EL spectrum of the cool WLED device. Inset is the optical image of a turned-on cool WLED. (e) EL spectrum of the WLED device. Inset is the optical image of a turned-on WLED. (f) EL spectrum of the warm WLED device. Inset is the optical image of a turned-on warm WLED. Dependence of (g) CCT and CRI of WLEDs varying with the thickness of N-GQDs film thickness. (h) EL spectra, (i) CRI and CCT of the WLED under different driven currents from 20 to 120 mA (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

head.ce:title: Solid-state fluorescent nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots with yellow emission for white light-emitting diodes

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Light-emitting graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are widely investigated due to their distinct merits. However, GQDs generally suffer from aggregation-induced luminescence quenching, which means they are highly emissive in a solution state with uniform dispersion but dramatically quenched in a solid or aggregated state. This problem significantly limits the application of GQDs, partially in the solid-state light-emitting devices. In this report, we successfully developed a simple and efficient hydrothermal method for the production of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with strong solid-state fluorescence (SSF) by using citric acid and o-Phenylenediamine as precursors. Under the 365 nm UV light illumination, the produced N-GQDs in an aqueous state exhibit blue color, with a quantum yield of 58%. As the concentration of N-GQDs increases, the photoluminescence exhibits an obvious red-shift from blue to yellow. For the N-GQDs in solid state, yellow luminescence with a high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 28% is achieved under the 405 nm excitation. Finally, via the simple adjustment of thickness and the concentration of N-GQDs in blue emitting InGaN chips, color converter is enabled by constructing white light-emitting diode (WLED) device with improved color rendering index (CRI) and correlated color temperature (CCT).

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) as the next-generation of solid-state light sources have attracted extensive attentions owing to their compact size, fast response, low power consumption, long operational life, and remarkable energy efficiency [1–3]. Commercial phosphors commonly used in WLEDs are rare-earth-based materials. However, the high cost of raw materials and the environmental damage caused by the mining process limit the widespread use of phosphors-based WLEDs [4]. Semiconductor nanocrystals, especially semiconductor quantum dots, have been exploited for the fabrication of WLEDs due to their high fluorescent quantum yield, narrow emission bandwidth, and resistance to the photobleaching [5,6]. However, the external quantum efficiency (EQE) of WLEDs based on semiconductor quantum dots is relatively low. For example, EQE of WLED with CdSe nanocrystal as the light-emitting layer is only 0.0013% [2]. Furthermore, the heavy metal components (such as Cd and Pb) may pose toxicity risks [3,7]. Consequently, alternative materials that can be easily prepared by green procedures are essential for the development of WLEDs.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, were first demonstrated in 2008 [8]. Because of their low toxicity, excellent biological compatibility, facile synthesis and unique physical nature [9], GQDs have been widely developed for the applications in bioimaging [10–12], photocatalysis [12,13], sensors [14,15], lasers [16,17], LEDs [18,19], and photovoltaic devices [20]. In terms of solid-state LEDs, some critical issues of GQDs, such as the difficulty of obtaining solid powder because of their hydroscopicity, and aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching (ACQ) in the solid state because of the excessive resonance energy transfer (RET) or direct π–π interactions, need to be addressed. Therefore, most recently applications of GQDs are based on their fluorescence in aqueous solution or liquid state, and only few study mentioned their synthesized GQDs has the property of solid-state fluorescence (SSF) [21]. These drawbacks may severely restrict the application of GQDs in some fields, especially in light emitting diodes, optoelectronic devices, luminescent solar concentrators, and other related, which generally require photoluminescence in the solid state [22]. To overcome this problem, several suitable matrices are utilized to ensure the monodispersity of GQDs in the solid state. For instance, Wang et al. realized solid state blue-emitting composite phosphors (PLQY of 58%) by embedding CDs into a polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane matrix [23]. Chen et al. applied polymer coating to prevent the solid state luminescence quenching of CDs [24]. Rogach et al. embedded CDs into polymethylmethacrylate to form luminescent phosphors, and combined the phosphors with ultraviolet (UV) chips to fabricate LEDs [25]. Although silica [26] and polymers [23] have been reported as solid matrices capable of supporting the incorporation of GQDs and enabling or adjusting their solid-state fluorescence, they cannot fundamentally prevent quenching by aggregation. Therefore, it is highly demanded to develop solid-state GQDs with long wavelength emissions and resistance to self-quenching.

In this work, we proposed a facile, high-yield and one-step hydrothermal method to prepare nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) by using citric acid and o-Phenylenediamine as starting materials. Under the 365 nm UV light, the prepared N-GQDs exhibit blue color in aqueous solution state, with a quantum yield of 58%. As the concentration of N-GQDs increase, a distinct red-shift of the photoluminescence of aqueous N-GQDs, from blue to yellow, is obtained. Under the 405 nm excitation, the solid state N-GQDs emit a yellow luminescence with a high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 28%. The N-GQDs phosphors exhibit good photostability and thermal stability. Based on these advantages, color converters for constructing white light-emitting diode devices with improved color rendering index and correlated color temperature are demonstrated via the simple adjustment of thickness and concentration of N-GQDs, which are integrated in blue emitting InGaN chips. This study not only provides a simple and convenient method for preparing solid-state fluorescent N-GQDs with self-quenching-resistant, but also demonstrates promising potentials of N-GQDs for building white light-emitting devices.

All chemical reagents are purchased as analytical grade without any further purification. Citric acid (CA), o-Phenylenediamine (OPD) and polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) are purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Water used throughout all experiments is purified by the Millipore system.

N-GQDs are synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. Citric acid (0.42 g, 2.0 mmol) and o-Phenylenediamine (0.324 g, 3.0 mmol) are dissolved in 75 mL deionized water, followed by the ultrasonic dispersion for 10 min. Then the mixture is transferred into a 100 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 16 h. After naturally cooling down to 25 °C, the obtained yellow solution dispersion is filtered off using a microporous membrane (0.22 µm) and the resultant solution was dialyzed for 72 h (retained molecular weight: 100–500 Da). N-GQDs are freeze-dried at −60 °C to obtain solids for the further use.

The multidimensional time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique to evaluate the fluorescence decay lifetime of N-GQDs with an excitation light of 375 nm. The decay traces for the N-GQDs are fitted using a double-exponential function based on nonlinear least-squares analysis in Eq. (1). The average lifetimes are calculated using Eq. (2) as follows [27].
(1)
Ι

=
B

e
− τ
1

τ
+
B
2
e
− τ
2
/
τ
(2)
τ
α ν e
=
B
1
τ
1
2
+
B
2
τ
2
2
B
1
τ
1
+
B
2
τ
2
where τ1 and τ2 represent the decay time constants, and B1 and B2 are the pre-exponential factors for the time resolved decay lifetime of τ1 and τ2, respectively.

Morphologies of the N-GQDs were characterized by using transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi H-8100) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Oxford Instruments, Cypher S). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS, PHI Quantera II system, Ulvac-PHI, INC, Japan) of N-GQDs were obtained with a PHI1580EXCA photoelectron spectrometer. A spectrometer (Bruker, WQF-410) was used to obtain Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of N-GQDs. Raman spectra (HORIBA Jobin Yvon HR800) were obtained using an Ar+ laser with the wavelength of 532 nm. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption properties of N-GQDs were characterized by a UV-5800 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were collected on a PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K.) at room temperature. The PL quantum yields (QYs) of N-GQDs were determined by comparing the integrated PL intensities (excited at 365 nm) and the absorbance values using quinine sulfate as the reference. A SCIENTZ-100F freeze dryer was used to obtain N-GQDs solid powder.

Firstly, 250 mg N-GQDs are mixed with 2 mL polydimethylsiloxane, and the following treatment by ultrasound will induce a complete dissolution. Then, the obtained mixture is spin-coated on a Si substrate and dried overnight under ambient circumstances to get composite films. We control the rotation speed to obtain composite films with different thicknesses. The thicknesses of the hybrid material are 0.4 ± 0.02, 0.8 ± 0.02, 1.2 ± 0.02 and 1.6 ± 0.02 mm for the rotation speed of 1200, 1000, 800 and 600 rpm/min, respectively.

The N-GQDs gel was drop-casted on the 460 nm emissive InGaN chip carefully, and cured at 80 °C for 1 h. In detail, 250 mg N-GQDs were mixed with 2 mL silicone elastomer base (A gel) and silicone elastomer curing agent (B gel) with 10:1 mass ratio, and then the mixture was drop-casted on the chip for the blue LED.

The experimental conditions including reaction time, the molar ratio of reactants and temperature are investigated to prepare N-GQDs with optimal optical properties. The fluorescence intensity of the prepared N-GQDs is increased when the reaction time enlarged from 8 to 16 h and then decreases with the further increase of the reaction time to 24 h (Fig. S1a). As the doping contents play an important role in regulating the fluorescence intensity of the prepared N-GQDs, the molar ratio of citric acid to o-Phenylenediamine is optimized. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs with different molar ratios and a constant reaction time of 16 h, is shown in Fig. S1b. The PL intensity of the N-GQDs reaches the maximum when the molar ratio of citric acid to o-Phenylenediamine is 2:3. As a result, the optimum molar ratio and reaction time of N-GQDs are determined to be 2:3 and 16 h, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs increases with the increase of the reaction temperature until reaching 150 °C (Fig. S1c). Consequently, the 150 °C is chosen as the optimal temperature. There is no change in the emission wavelength of N-GQDs when the reaction temperature lies in the range of 130–180 °C (Fig. S1d).

Fig. 1a shows the schematic of the fabrication process of producing N-GQDs via the facile hydrothermal method, with citric acid and o-Phenylenediamine as the precursors. A yellow-light solid sample is obtained by dialysis and freeze-drying, which is then re-dispersed in water. Notably, as the concentration of N-GQDs increases, distinct red-shift of the photoluminescence of N-GQDs, from blue to yellow, is observed. The morphology of N-GQDs is characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM). The TEM image in Fig. 1b shows the uniformly dispersed N-GQDs with a lateral size distribution of about 1–7 nm, and the average diameter is about 3.5 nm. The high-resolution TEM (top inset of Fig. 1b) of a typical N-GQDs shows that the lattice spacing is 0.24 nm corresponding to the (1120) lattice fringes of graphitic carbon [28,29]. The Fast Fourier transform (FFT, Fig. S2) of the N-GQDs shows a standard six-fold symmetry, demonstrating a good crystalline quality of the synthesized N-GQDs [30]. AFM results presented in Fig. 1c and d further confirm the uniform N-GQDs dispersion, and the average height is found as about 0.7 nm, indicating 1–2 layer of graphene in terms of the thickness of N-GQDs [31]. From the X-ray diffraction (XRD) result shown in Fig. S3a, the broad peak centered at around 21° indicates that the synthesized N-GQDs have a graphitic structure [32], which is consistent with the HRTEM image. The ratio of the disordered (D) band (at 1382 cm−1) to the crystalline (G) band (at 1558 cm−1), i.e., ID/IG, is found to be 0.59, as demonstrated by the Raman scattering result shown in Fig. S3b, indicating that N-GQDs possess highly ordered graphitization structure [33].

To investigate the chemical bonds of functional groups, FT-IR spectrum of N-GQDs is measured, as shown in Fig. 1e. Similar to previous studies [34,35], peaks at 1102 cm−1, 1258 cm−1, 1582 cm−1, 1703 cm−1, 3210 cm−1, and 3412 cm−1 are assigned as stretching vibrations of C‒O, C‒N, C˭O, C˭C, N‒H and O‒H, respectively. It is clearly shown that nitrogen is successfully doped in N-GQDs. According to the presented results, the possible structure of the N-GQDs is speculated in inset of Fig. 1e, with the existence of some related functional groups on the surface, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino. The surface states of N-GQDs are further investigated by X-ray photoelectron energy spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS spectrum of the N-GQDs (Fig. S4) shows the presence of C, N, and O with the atomic percentage of 74.35%, 10.72%, and 14.93%, respectively, and the corresponding C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s peaks are located at 285.5, 399.6, and 531.5 eV, respectively. The high-resolution of C 1s spectrum contains several peaks 284.8, 285.7, 286.8 and 288.3 eV, as shown in Fig. 1f, denoting the presence of sp2 C of C˭C groups and sp3 C of C‒N, C‒O and C˭O groups [26,36]. In addition, two dominant peaks emerge from the XPS spectrum of the N 1s, as shown in Fig. 1g, which are attributed to C‒N (399.4 eV) and N‒H (401.2 eV) [37], respectively. Therefore, it is concluded that nitrogen atoms are successfully doped into the N-GQDs. Fig. 1h shows the high resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s, and two peaks at 531.6 and 533.2 eV are observed, which represents C˭O and C‒O bands [38], respectively.

The optical property of as-prepared N-GQDs is identified by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. Fig. 2a shows the typical UV–vis absorption spectrum (blue trace) and PL excitation (red trace) and emission (light blue trace) spectra of the N-GQDs. It is shown that N-GQDs has a broad absorption in the UV region, with three peaks centered at 243, 272 and 368 nm, which are ascribed to the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, n-π * transition of C˭O, and conjugated C‒N, respectively [39–41]. The N-GQDs show a maximum excitation wavelength and a maximum emission wavelength of 365 and 440 nm, respectively. Then, a series of PL spectra under different excitation wavelength from 340 to 390 nm are measured. As shown in Fig. 2b, the N-GQDs show excitation-independent behavior as the increase of the excitation wavelength. Moreover, the average fluorescence lifetime of N-GQDs solution is calculated as 3.17 ns, as show in Fig. 2c. Interestingly, the N-GQDs solutions and powders exhibit different color emission under UV the light (Fig. 2d). Fig. S5a shows optical images of the N-GQDs solution and powder under the sunlight. Using quinine sulfate as a reference chromophore, the quantum yield (QY) of N-GQDs is obtained as 58% under the excitation at 365 nm (Fig. S5b). This high QY value may be ascribed to the nitrogen doping [42]. In detail, a dilute (1 mg/mL) N-GQDs solution shows an emission peak at 440 nm, which is independent of the excitation wavelength, as demonstrated in Fig. 2e. For the N-GQDs powder, however, a bright yellow fluorescence, corresponding to the emission peak at 565 nm, is found (see Fig. 2f). The PLQY of the powder is estimated to be 28%. The Fig. 2g shows normalized PL spectra of N-GQDs solutions at different concentrations, and the corresponding fluorescent images are shown in Fig. 2h. Combined with the result shown in Fig. S6, N-GQDs exhibit a significant concentration-dependent luminescence. As the N-GQDs concentration increases from 20 mg/mL to 200 mg/mL, the emission peak appears at much larger wavelength, with a distinct red-shifts from 463 nm to 562 nm.

According to the energy transfer effect, energy splitting and broadening accompany with the N-GQDs aggregation, due to the increased concentration, thus leading to a reduced energy band gap [43–45]. The concentration-dependent behavior from N-GQDs could be attributed to their interactions, and the corresponding schematic is shown in Fig. 3a. When the electrons in ground state absorb photons and transit to the excited state, they may shift to another lower vibrational level of excited state via internal conversion and vibration relaxation, then fluorescence is released when the electrons return to the ground state. Fig. 3b shows the absorption spectra of N-GQDs solutions with different concentrations. It is found that the dilute N-GQDs solution (1 mg/mL) possesses a peak at 368 nm, arising from the n-π* transitions. A new absorption peak appears at 455 nm as the concentration increases. For better understanding, the absorption spectra are converted to Tauc plots of (αhν)2 versus hν to estimate the optical band gaps (Eg) of these N-GQDs, in which α, h, and ν are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and light frequency, respectively [46]. The optical energy band gap (resulting from the absorption edge in the absorption spectrum) of the N-GQDs are estimated to be 3.18, 3.1, 3.06, 3.03, 2.99, 2.96, 2.92, and 2.79 eV, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3c. Fig. 3d shows the energy levels of N-GQDs at different concentrations. As the concentration increases, the Eg of N-GQDs continues to decrease. Therefore, as the concentration increases, energy transfer may occur, resulting in the red-shifted light emission of N-GQDs from blue to yellow. In Fig. 3e, the PL spectrum with broad peak at 562 nm overlaps with the absorption band at 455 nm, implying that the yellow light emission originates from the transition of the absorption band at 455 nm rather than the band at 368 nm. In short, the blue emission is contributed by the monodispersed N-GQDs, whereas the yellow emission is attributed to the aggregated N-GQDs. This indicates the effective resonant energy transfer (RET) from dispersed N-GQDs to agglomerated ones. To verify the occurrence of the RET, fluorescence decay properties of N-GQDs with different concentrations are measured, as shown in Fig. 3f. The excited-state lifetime of N-GQDs monitored at 450 nm gradually decreased from 3.65 to 1.02 ns as the concentration increased. It means that the RET competes with radiative transition and then shortens the fluorescence lifetime [47,48]. Therefore, the red-shift behavior in aggregated N-GQDs compared to the dispersed N-GQDs is attributed to RET, because the short emission wavelength is weakened due to the RET, and as a result, the longer emission wavelength is comparatively strengthened. Based on the above results, a possible mechanism for self-quenching-resistant and aggregation-induced red-shift is shown in Fig. 3g. As the concentration increases, monodispersed N-GQDs gather into aggregates, enabling the red-shift emission through RET.

Outstanding thermostability of the phosphors for the application in LEDs is required. Therefore, the TG analysis is conducted under a nitrogen atmosphere to evaluate thermostability of N-GQDs. The results shown in Fig. S7a reveal a weight loss of 7% from N-GQDs powder at 200 °C, suggesting that they are thermostable enough to be applied in LEDs. Furthermore, none fluorescence attenuation is observed for N-GQDs phosphor when the heating temperature rises from 20 °C to 120 °C, as shown in Fig. S7b. The fluorescence intensity varying with the UV irradiation (365 nm lamp) time is shown in Fig. S7c, and almost no reduction in intensity is found, suggesting the superior UV-resistant ability of the N-GQDs phosphor. Fig. S7d shows fluorescence intensity variation of the N-GQDs phosphor versus the storage time, indicating that a good fluorescence stability of the N-GQDs phosphor. Fig. S8 shows that the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs phosphor maintains a stable excitation response after thirty reuse cycles.

Thanks to the unique optical properties and excellent stability, the N-GQDs phosphors can be employed to generate the WLED. Fig. 4a displays a schematic diagram of the WLED, consisting of a blue LED chip with 460 nm emission and a layer of N-GQDs powder. First, N-GQDs are mixed with polydimethylsiloxane, then the mixture is drop-casted on the blue-emitting chip as the conversion layer. The integrated device is annealed at 80 °C for 1 h to solidify. The electroluminescence (EL) spectrum of the naked blue LED chip shown in Fig. 4b displays the emission band centered individually at 460 nm. A bright blue light can be emitted at a driving voltage of 3 V, as shown in the inset of Fig. 4b. According to the colorimetry theory, the commission international de lEclairage (CIE) color coordinate of the LEDs fabricated by the N-GQDs phosphors and InGaN chips is shown in Fig. 4c with the red curved line, suggesting the presence of reabsorption. When the mass ratio of the N-GQDs phosphors and polydimethylsiloxane is 0.25:1 wt%, only cool WLEDs with CIE color coordinate of (0.25, 0.31) and CCT of 11843 K is obtained (Fig. 4d), which is mainly because a small number of phosphors cannot sufficiently convert the blue light emitted from the InGaN chip (the inset of Fig. 4d). Then, the mass ratio is increased to 1:1 wt%, the fabricated WLED is capable of emitting a bright white light at a driving voltage of 3 V, as exhibited in the inset of Fig. 4e, with CIE color coordinate and CCT of (0.33, 0.36) and 5723 K, respectively. Further increasing the mass ratio to 1.5:1 wt%, the LED exhibits warm yellow emissive color (inset Fig. 4f), with CIE coordinate and color temperature of (0.40, 0.41) and 3820 K respectively, as presented in Fig. 4f. We fabricate freestanding films of N-GQDs using polydimethylsiloxane as the supporting matrix. Fig. 4g shows the changes in color temperature and color rendering index varying with the film thickness. The EL spectra of the WLED under different driven currents from 20 to 120 mA are shown in Fig. 4h. As the driven current increases from 20 to 120 mA, the CCT and CRI also show a slight change from 5675 to 5783 K and 87.5–91, respectively (Fig. 4i). The above results reveal that the WLEDs based on our synthesized N-GQDs have the potential to be utilized as the high-performance lighting source towards WLEDs-based illumination applications.

In conclusion, N-GQDs are synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method of citric acid and o-Phenylenediamine compounds, with unique concentration-dependent and tunable fluorescence properties. With increasing the N-GQDs concentration, monodispersed N-GQDs gather into aggregates, enabling a significant red-shift emission through RET. PL obtained from the N-GQDs can be continuously tuned from 440 to 562 nm as the concentration increases from 1 to 200 mg/mL. The obtained N-GQDs solid powder shows a bright yellow emission with a PLQY of 28%, as well as good photostability and thermal stability. Based on these advantages, color converters for constructing white light-emitting diode devices with improved color rendering index and correlated color temperature are demonstrated via the simple adjustment of thickness and concentration of N-GQDs, which are integrated in blue emitting InGaN chips. This study not only provides a simple and convenient method for preparing solid-state fluorescent N-GQDs with self-quenching-resistant, but also demonstrates promising potentials of N-GQDs for building white light-emitting devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (Nos. 11704204, 61604084 and 51602056), K.C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo under Grant (No. 2017A610104).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.synthmet.2021.116787.
